content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 13, 2012

Ms. Anita D. Stearns Mayo
Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, LLP
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA 94120-7880

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file no. I-11-242

Dear Ms. Stearns Mayo:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry asks general questions of law and does not present specific facts concerning a named corporation and its expenditures on a particular ballot measure, we are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.²

FACTS

Pillsbury Winthrop represents numerous clients who, from time to time, make independent expenditures to support or oppose state or local ballot measures. Many of these clients are corporations which qualify as state major donor committees.

When a corporation which qualifies as a state major donor committee makes independent expenditures to support or oppose a local ballot measure, the corporation becomes subject to additional disclosure and advertising disclaimer requirements. You have the following general questions concerning corporate political activity under the Act.

1. When a corporation's internal employees spend more than ten percent of their compensated time in one month engaged in political activities expressly advocating the support or opposition of a ballot measure, the corporation must report the portion of the employees' salaries spent on such activities as independent expenditures. However, the

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 2

corporation is not required to disclose the names, addresses and the specific salaries of those
employees. Although a 1979 opinion (In re Nielsen,5 FPPC Ops 18) stated that such disclosure
is required, the current FPPC Campaign Disclosure Manual 6, page 3-7, provides that such
itemized disclosure is not required.

Regulation 18423 addresses when an employer's payments for an employee's personal
services count as contributions and expenditures under the Act. Regulation 18423 provides:

"(a) The payment of salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, or other
compensation by an employer to an employee who spends more than 10% of his
compensated time in any one month rendering services for political purposes is a
contribution, as defined in Section 82015 and Regulation 18215, or an
expenditure, as defined in Section 82025 and Regulation 18225, by the employer
if:
(1) The employee renders services at the request or direction of the employer;
(2) The employee, with consent of the employer, is relieved of any normal
working responsibilities related to his employment in order to render the personal
services, unless the employee engages in political activity on bona fide, although
compensable, vacation time or pursuant to a uniform policy allowing employees
to engage in political activity.
(b) Personal services are rendered for political purposes if they are carried on
for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters
for or against the nomination or election of one or more candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure, and include but are not limited to:
(1) Personal services received by or made at the behest of a candidate or
committee by an employee; and
(2) Hours spent developing or distributing communications that expressly
advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure.
(c) The amount of the contribution or expenditure reportable pursuant to this
regulation is the pro-rata portion of the gross salary, reimbursement for personal
expenses or compensation attributable to the time spent on political activity. An
in-kind contribution of the services of salaried personnel to a committee and the
expenditure by the person making the salary payment are considered to be made
on the payroll date of the salaried personnel."

The advice in FPPC disclosure Manual 6 is generally in agreement with the 1979 Nielsen
Opinion; the names, addresses and the specific salaries of the employees spending more than ten
percent of their compensated time are not required to be disclosed, unless the employee is
specifically hired to work on the campaign.

In re Nielsen, 5 FPPC Ops. 18, involved five major donors: R.J. Reynolds Tobacco
Company, Liggett & Myers Tobacco Company, Phillip Morris, Inc., Brown & Williamson
Tobacco Corporation, and the Tobacco Institute. Each of the major donors opposed the passage
of a statewide ballot measure, Proposition 5 on the November 7, 1978 ballot, and had employees
who spent sufficient amount of time opposing the passage of Proposition 5 so that the value of

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 3


their time constituted an in-kind contribution to the committee opposing the measure. The
question presented was whether a major donor committee making the contribution to the ballot
measure has to report the name, address, and salary of the employees working on the measure.
The opinion found that a major donor committee making the contribution is not required to
report the name, address and salary of the employees whose services constitute an in-kind
contribution if the employees are regular employees of the major donor committee.

The Opinion reasoned as follows:

"Requiring the disclosure of names of the employees of major donors would
not, in most instances, serve any of these purposes. No inference may be
drawn about a candidate or ballot measure since, by participating in
campaigns, employees of major donor committees may not be demonstrating
any personal commitment to, or support of, a candidate or ballot measure, but
rather are merely carrying out their employer's instructions. Thus, while
disclosure of the major donor's identity would clearly serve the purposes of
the Act, disclosure of the individual employees' names would provide little
additional information to the voters. The situation would be different, of
course, if the employees had specifically applied, and had been hired, to
engage in political campaign activities. In that case, disclosure of employees'
names might well assist voters in placing the candidate or ballot measure in
the political spectrum and in identifying individuals to whom a candidate is
more likely to be responsive once in office.

Another factor we must consider in resolving the ambiguity of Section
84210(h)³ is that the disclosure of the names, addresses and salaries may
affect the privacy interests of the major donor employees. Before such
interests are affected, we must ensure that requiring disclosure of the
employees' names, addresses and salaries will truly promote the purposes of
the Act.

These considerations lead us to conclude that in the case where a major
donor committee makes an in-kind contribution of employees' services, the
purposes of the Act are best served by disclosure of the amount of money
spent, the time involved and the fact that the employees' services were a
contribution to the recipient committee.

In conclusion, pursuant to Section 84210(h), the entities must report the
expenditure for the employees' services as a payment to CCS [Californians
for Common Sense] and include a description of the consideration. The
description of the consideration should state the number of employees and
days on which the employees performed campaign services for CCS. Under 2

___________________________
³ Section 84210(h) was the expenditure reporting section currently contained in Section 84211(k).

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 4

Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423, only those employees who spend more than
10 percent of their compensated time performing campaign services will be
includable. Would disclosure, including the names, addresses and salaries of the
employees, would have to be made only if the entities had specifically hired
individuals to work on the campaign."

2. When a corporation pays its employees this year to draft brochures urging voters to
support or oppose a ballot measure, but the brochures will not be distributed until next year, is it
correct that the expenses incurred (salaries and other costs) by the corporation to develop the
brochures will not qualify as independent expenditures until the date the brochures are
distributed to the public? Thus, the expenses are not reported until the reporting period
covering the date the brochures are distributed to the public?

We are not able to provide advice on this question without any specific facts identifying
the name of the corporation, the ballot measure involved, whether the corporation is coordinating
its activities with a ballot measure committee, and a fuller description of the work being
performed by the corporation's employees on the measure. These facts are necessary to
determine whether the payments for employee services are independent expenditures or in-
contributions to a ballot measure committee. You are welcome to write in providing more facts
on this question.

3. Since a local ballot measure committee must file semi-annual, pre-election, and
quarterly statements, is it accurate that a state major donor committee which makes independent
expenditures to support or oppose that ballot measure committee must similarly file Form 465 on
a semi-annual, pre-election and quarterly basis, but only if the state major donor committee has
made independent expenditures during the period covered by the foregoing statements?

This understanding of when to file the supplemental independent expenditure report is
correct. The purpose of the supplemental independent expenditure report is to get information
about independent expenditures of $1,000 or more made by any candidate or committee,
reported in the jurisdiction where the affected candidate or measure is on the ballot.

The requirement for the supplemental independent expenditure report is found in Section
84203.5, which provides in part:

"(a) In addition to any campaign statements required by this article, if a
candidate or committee has made independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year to support or oppose a
candidate, a measure or qualification of a measure, it shall file independent
expenditure reports at the same time, covering the same periods, and in the
places where the candidate or committee would be required to file campaign
statements under this article, as if it were formed or existing primarily to
support or oppose the candidate or measure or qualification of the measure.

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 5

No independent expenditure report need be filed to cover a period for which
there has been no activity to report." (Emphasis added.)

Under this provision, a major donor committee making independent expenditures on a
ballot measure will file the Form 465 at the same times that and in the same location where the
ballot measure committee supported or opposed by the independent expenditures is required to
file. The major donor committee making independent expenditures will also disclose the
independent expenditures on its next regular campaign report (Form 461).

4. Regulation 18225(b)(4)(B) exempts a corporation's regularly published newsletter
from the definition of a reportable expenditure when the circulation is limited to, among others,
affiliated individuals. Does the term "affiliated individuals" include a corporation's board of
directors?

Regulation 18225(b)(4)(B) provides:

"(4) Notwithstanding the provisions of this subsection, the term expenditure
does not include costs incurred for communications which expressly advocate
the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or
candidates or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
measure or measures by:
...
(B) A regularly published newsletter or regularly published periodical,
other than those specified in (b)(4)(A), whose circulation is limited to an
organization's members, employees, shareholders, other affiliated individuals
and those who request or purchase the publication. This paragraph applies
only to the costs regularly incurred in publishing and distributing the
newsletter or periodical. If additional costs are incurred because the
newsletter or periodical is issued on other than its regular schedule, expanded
in circulation, or substantially altered in style, size or format, the additional
costs are expenditures." (Emphasis added.)

We agree that with respect to a corporation, the term "affiliated individuals" includes a
corporation's board of directors.

5. With respect to the various advertisement disclaimers required by Chapter 4 of the
Political Reform Act, Article 5, Advertisements, and its related regulations, is it correct that only
those provisions regulating broadcasts and mass mailings apply to a major donor committee that
makes independent expenditures to support or oppose a ballot measure? Based on the recently
issued Rios Advice Letter, No. A-11-181, the term "broadcast" in Section 84506 has been
interpreted to include electronic communications. As a consequence, electronic media
advertisements are now subject to Article 5 and its related regulations. Accordingly, is it
accurate that Article 5 and its related regulations, do not apply to advertisements placed in other
media (billboards, posters, yard signs, door hangers, bumper stickers, campaign buttons, faxes

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 6

and newspapers) by a major donor committee that makes independent expenditures to support or
oppose a local ballot measure?

A corporation which qualifies as a major donor committee, that is making independent
expenditures to support or oppose a ballot measure, must comply with the advertisement
disclosure rules in 84506. Section 84506 provides:

"(a) A broadcast or mass mailing advertisement supporting or opposing a
candidate or ballot measure, that is paid for by an independent expenditure,
shall include a disclosure statement that identifies both of the following:
(1) The name of the committee making the independent expenditure.
(2) The names of the persons from whom the committee making the
independent expenditure has received its two highest cumulative contributions
of fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) or more during the 12-month period prior
to the expenditure. . . ."4 (Emphasis added.)

An "advertisement" is defined in Sections 84501 and Regulation 18450.1, copy enclosed.

Section 84506 of the Act does not expressly require that an independent expenditure
advertisement supporting or opposing a ballot measure that is placed in a newspaper, other print
media, or a billboard, have the advertising disclosure statement. However, the FPPC
recommends that a committee place "paid for by committee name" disclaimers on all campaign
materials, including any independent expenditure ads supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

6. Is it correct that a corporation which qualifies as a major donor committee that makes
independent expenditures to support or oppose a local ballot measure is not required to include
any disclaimers on advertisements that are sent to the corporation's employees, shareholders, or
families of the employees and shareholders?

The definition of advertisement in Section 84501 and Regulation 18450.1 specifically
excludes a communication from an organization, other than a political party, to its members.

Section 84501 states:

"(a) 'Advertisement' means any general or public advertisement which is
authorized and paid for by a person or committee for the purpose of
supporting or opposing a candidate for elective office or a ballot measure or
ballot measures.
(b) 'Advertisement' does not include a communication from an
organization other than a political party to its members, a campaign button

4 Regulation 18450.4 states that: "(a) [t]he disclosure requirements of Sections 84503 and 84506(a)(2)
shall not apply to general purpose committees, as defined by Section 82027.5." The top two $50,000 donor
disclaimer does not apply to major donor committees, because they are general purpose committees and they do not
receive contributions from others.

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 7

smaller than 10 inches in diameter, a bumper sticker smaller than 60 square
inches, or other advertisement as determined by regulations of the
Commission." (Emphasis added.)

In addition, Regulation 18450.1 provides in part:

"(b) In addition to the exempted communications in subdivision (b) of
Section 84501, none of the following are an "advertisement":
. . .
(2) A communication from an organization to its members, other than a
communication from a political party to its members. . . ."

This provision applies to communications where the corporation's employees,
shareholders, or their families will know that the corporation is communicating with them via the
employee newsletter, or a letter to shareholders, so an additional disclaimer stating that the
communication is from the corporation would be superfluous and is not required.

7. Since the definition of advertisement in Regulation 18450.1(a)(3) includes an
electronic message delivered to more than 200 recipients, is it correct that an electronic media
advertisement forwarded via an electronic message (e-mail) is not required to include any
disclaimers unless the electronic message is distributed to more than 200 recipients?

No, the electronic media advertisement is required to have the disclaimer specified by
Section 84506, requiring the committee name.

Section 18450.1 defines an "advertisement" in part as follows:

"(a) An advertisement as defined in Section 84501 includes but is not
limited to the following:
(1) Programming received by a television or radio;
(2) A communication as described in subdivision (a) of Section 84501 that
is placed in broadcast, print or electronic media;
(A) An electronic media advertisement means an advertisement, logo, icon,
writing, image, recording, or other data transmitted, distributed, posted,
broadcast, or displayed electronically. This includes, but is not limited to
advertisements in electronic messages, electronic message attachments, text
messages, or advertisements that appear on Internet web pages, blogs, mobile
devices, or other electronic communication systems.
(3) A telephone, facsimile, or electronic message that is not solicited by the
recipient and is intended for delivery in substantially similar form to more
than 200 recipients. For purposes of this paragraph, when a committee sends
a message to 200 or fewer recipients and the message is "forwarded" to other
persons by a recipient, the message sent by the committee is not an
advertisement by that committee unless the recipient forwarded the message at

File No. I-11-242
Page No. 8

the behest of the committee and more than 200 total recipients received the
message."

Paragraph (a)(3) applies to an email communication, such as a message from a committee
that asks recipients to "vote for Measure X." Such an electronic mail message, that is not
solicited by the recipient and is intended for delivery in substantially similar form to more than
200 recipients, must have a disclaimer stating the committee name.

Paragraph (a)(2) applies to an electronic media advertisement. If a committee places a
video advertisement on a website against a measure, the advertisement is required to have a
disclaimer citing the committee name. If the committee forwards this video advertisement via
email, the video advertisement must retain the disclaimer.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.facts:
Pillsbury Winthrop represents numerous clients who, from time to time, make
independent expenditures to support or oppose state or local ballot measures. Many of these
clients are corporations which qualify as state major donor committees.
When a corporation which qualifies as a state major donor committee makes independent
expenditures to support or oppose a local ballot -eurrr", the corporation becomes subject to
additional disclosure and advertising disclaimer requirements. You have the following general
questions conceming corporate political activity unter the Act.
l. When a corporation's internal employees spend more than ten percent of thetr
compensated time in one month engaged in political activities expressly idvocating the sypport
or opposition of a ballot measure, the corporation mttst report tie pro'rata portion of the
employees' salaries spent on such acttvities as independent expendtnrr"r. However, the
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through 91014. All statutory

corporation is not reEired to dtsclose the names, addresses and the specific salaries of those
employees. Although a 1979 opinton (ln re Nielsen,S FPPC Ops 18) stated that such disclosure
is required, the current FPPC Campaign Disclosure Marutal6, page 3-7, provides that such
itemized disclosure is not reEtired.
Regulation 18423 addresses when an employer's payments for an employee's personal
services count as contributions and expenditures under the Act. Regulation lS4i3 p.ouidest
"(a) The payment of salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, or other
compensation by an employer to an employee who spends more than l07o of his
compensated time in any one month rendering services for political purposes is a
contribution, as defined in section 82015 and Regulation lg2l5, or an
expenditure, as defined in Section 82025 and Regulation 18225, by the employer
if:
( 1) The employee renders services at the request or direction of the employer;
(2) The employee, with consent of the employer, is relieved of any normal
working responsibilities related to his employment in order to render the personal
services, unless the employee engages in political activity on bona fide, aithough
compensable, vacation time or pursuant to a uniform policy allowing employees
to engage in political activity.
(b) Personal services are rendered forpolitical purposes ifthey are carried on
for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters
for or against the nomination or'election of one or more candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure, and include but are not limited to:
(1) Personal services received by or made at the behest of a candidate or
committee by an employee; and
(2) Hours spent developing or distributing communications that expressly .
advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure.
(c) The amount of the contribution or expenditure reportable pursuant to this
regulation is the pro-rata portion of the gross salary, reimbursement for personal
expenses or compensation attributable to the time spent on political activity. An
in-kind contribution of the services of salaried personnel to a committee and the
expenditure by the person making the salary payment are considered to.be made
on the payroll date of the salaried personnel.,'
The advice in FPPC disclosure Manual 6 is generally in agreement with the lgTgNielsen
Opinion; the names, addresses and the specific salaries of the ernployees spending more than ten
percent of their compensated time are not required to be disclosed, unless ihe employee is
specifically hired to work on the campaign.
In re Nielsen, 5 FPPC Clpt. tS, involved five major donors: R.J. Reynolds Tobacco
Company, Liggett & Myers Tobacco Company, Phillip Morris, Inc., Brown& Williamson
Tobacco Colporation, and the Tobacco Institute. Each of the major donors opposed the passage
of a statewide ballot measure, Proposition 5 on the November 7, lg78 ballot, and had employees
who spent sufficient amount of time opposing the passage of Proposition 5 so that the value of


File No. l-lL-242
their time constituted an in-kind contribution to the committee opposing the measure. The

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: was whether a major donor committee making the contribution to the ballot
measure had to report the name, address, and salary of the employees working on the measlrre.
The opinion found that a major donor committee making the contribution is not required to
report the name, address and salary of the employees whose services constitute an in-kind
contribution if the employees are regular employees of the major donor committee.
The Opinion reasoned as follows:
"Requiring the disclosure of names of the employees of major donors would
not, in most instances, serye any of these puqposes. No inference may be
drawn about a candidate or ballot measure since, by participating in
campaigns, employees of major donor committees may not be demonstrating
any personal commitment to, or support of, a candidate or ballot measure, but
rather are merely carrying out their employer's instructions. Thus, while
disclosure of the major donor's identity would clearly serve the purposes of
the Act, disclosure of the individual employees' narnes would piovide little
additional information to the voters. The situation would be different, of
course, if the employees had specifically applied, and had been hired, to
engage in political campaign activities. In that case, disclosure of employees,
narnes might well assist voters in placing the candidate or ballot measure in
the political spectrum and in identifying individuals to whom a candidate is
more likely to be responsive once in office.
Another factor we must consider in resolving the ambiguity of Section
s4210(h)3 is that the disclosure of the nrunes, addresses and ialaries may
affect the privacy interests of the major donor employees. Before such
interests are affected, we must ensure that requiring disclosure of the
employees' names, addresses and salaries will truly promote the purposes of
the Act.
These considerations lead us to conclude that in the case where a major
donor committee makes an in-kind contribution of employees' servicei, the
purposes of the Act are best served by disclosure of the amount of money
spent, the time involved and the fact that the employees' seryices were a
contribution to the recipient committee.
In conclusion, pursuant to Section 84210(h), the entities must report the
expenditure for the employeeg' services as a payment to CCS [Californians
for common Sensel and include a description of the consideration. The
description of the consideration should state the number of employees and
days on which the employees performed campaign services for ccs. tJnder 2
3 Section 84210(h) was the expenditure reporting section currently contained in Section 8421l(k).


File No. I-ll-242
cal. Adm. code section 18423, only those employees who spend more than
10 percent of their compensated time performing campaign services will be
includable. Full disclosure, including the names, addresses and salaries of the
employees, would have to be made only if the entities had specifically hired
individuals to work on the campaign."
2. When a corporation pays its employees this year to draft brochures urging voters to
support or oppose a ballot'measure, but the brochures will not be dtstributed until next year, is tt
correct that the expenses incurred (salaries and other costs) by the corporation to develop the
brochures will not Emlify as independent expendimres until the date the brochures are
distributed to the public? Thus, the expenses are not reported until the reporttng period
covering the date the brochures are distributed to the publtc?
We are not able to provide advice on this question without any specific facts identifying
the name of the corporation, the ballot measure involved, whether the corporation is coordinating
its activities with a ballot measure committee, and a fuller description of the work being
performed by the corporation's employees on the measure. These facts are necessary to
determine whether the payments for employee services are independent expenditurei or in-
contributions to a ballot measure committee. You are welcome to write in providing more facts
on this question.
3. Since a local ballot measure committee mustfile semi-annual, pre-election, and
quarterly statements, ts it accurate that a state major donor committee which makes tndependent
expenditures to suppofi or oppose that ballot measure committee must simtlarly file Form 465 on
a semi-annual, pre-election and quarterly basts, but only if the state major donor committee has
made independent expenditttres during the period covered by theforegoing statements?
This understanding of when to file the supplemental independent expenditure report is
correct. The pulpose of the supplemental independent expenditure report iJto get information
about independent expenditures of $1,000 or more made by any candidate or co-mmittee,
reported in the jurtsdiction where the affected candidate or measure is on the ballot.
The requirement for the supplemental independent expenditure report is found in Section
84203.5, which provides in part:
"(a) In addition to any campaign statements required by this article, if a
candidate or committee has made independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year to support or oppose a
candidate, a measure or qualification of a'measure, it shall file independent
expenditure reports ot tlrc scune time, covering the scune periocls, aricl in the
places where the candidate or committee would be required tofile campaign
statements under this article, as if it were formed or existing primarily to
sLlpport or oppose the candidate or measure or qualification of the measure.


File No. I-ll-242
No independent expenditure report need be filed to cover a period for which
there has been no activity to report." (Emphasis added.)
Under this provision, a major donor committee making independent expenditures on a
ballot measure will file the Form 465 at the same times that and in the same location where the
ballot measure committee supported or opposed by the independent expenditures is required to
file. The major donor committee making independent expenditures will also disclose the
independent expenditures on its next regular campaign report (Form 461).
4. Regulatton 18225(bX4XB) exempts a corporation's regularly published newsletter
from the definition of a reportable expenditure when the circulation is limited to, among others,
ffiliated individuals. Does the term "ffiliated individuals" include a corporation's board of
directors?
Re gulation 18225 (b)(4)(B ) provides :
"(4) Notwithstanding the provisions of this subsection, the term expenditure
does not include costs incurred for communications which expressly advocate
the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or
candidates or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
measure or measures by:
(B) A regularly published newsletter or regularly published periodical,
other than those specified in (bX4XA), whose circulation is limited to an
organization's members, employees, shareholders, other ffiliated individuals
and those who request or purchase the publication. This paragraph applies
only to the costs regularly incurred in publishing and distributing the
newsletter or periodical. If additional costs are incurred because the
newsletter or periodical is issued on other than its regular schedule, expanded
in circulation, or substantially altered in style, size or format, the additional
costs are expenditures." (Fmphasis added.)
We agree that with respect to a corporation, the term "affiliated individuals" includes a
corporation's board of directors
5. With respect to the various advertisement disclaimers required by Chapter 4 of the
Political Reform Act, Article 5, Advertisements, and its related regulations, is it correct that only
those provisions regulating broadcasts and mass mailings apply to a major donor commtttee that
,nhkes intlepentlent ,*p"rrdintres to .trtpport or oppose ,ibnltot measttre? Basecl on the recently
issued Rios Advice Letter, No. A-l 1-181, the terml"'broadcast" in Section 84506 has been
interpreted to include electronic communications. As a consequence, electronic media
advertisements are now subiect to Article 5 and its related regulations. Accordingly, is it
accurate that Article 5 and its related regulalions, do not apply to advertisements placed in other
media (billboards, posters, yard signs, door hangers, bumper stickers, campaign buttons, foxes


File No. I-ll-242
and newspapers) by a major donor committee that makes independent expenditttres to support or
oppose a local ballot measure?
A corporation which qualifies as a major donor committee, that is making independent
expenditures to support or oppose a ballot measure, must comply with the advertisement
disclosure rules in 84506. Section 84506 provides:
"(a) A broadcast or mass mailing advertisement supporting or opposing a
candidate or ballot measure, that is paid for by an independent expenditure,
shall include a disclosure statement that identifies both of the following:
(l) The name of the committee making the independent expenditure.
(2) The names of the persons from whom the committee making the
independent expenditure has received its two highest cumulative contributions
of fifty thousand dollars (950,000) or more during the l2-month period prior
to the expenditure. . . ."4 (Emphasis added.)
An "advertisement" is defined in Sections 84501 and Regulation 18450.1, copy enclosed.
Section 84506 of the Act does not expressly require that an independent expenditure
advertisement supporting or opposing a ballot measure that is placed in a newspaper, other print
media, or a billboard, have the advertising disclosure statement. However, the FppC
recommends that a committee place "paid for by committee name" disclaimers on all campaign
materials, including any independent expenditure ads supporting or opposing a ballot *"urur".
6. Is b correct that a corporation which qualifies as a major donor committee that makes
independent expendttures to sltpport or oppose a local ballot measure is not required to include
any dtsclaimers on advertisements that are sent to the corporation's employees, shareholders, or
families of 
.the 
employees and shareholders?
The definition of advertisement in Section 84501 and Regulation 18450.1 specifically
excludes a communication from an organization, other than a political party, to its members.
Section 84501 states:
"(a) 'Advertisement' means any general or public advertisement which is
authorized and paid for by a person or committee for the purpose of
supporting or opposing a candidate for elective office or a ballot measure or
ballot measures.
(b) 'Advertisement' tloes not include a commupiccttionfrom cm
organigfiion other than a political party to its rnembers, a campaign button
a Regulation 18450.4 states that: "(a) [t]he disclosure requirements of Sections 84503 and g4506(aX2)
shall not apply to general purpose committees, as defined by Section 82027.5.* The top two $50,000 donor
disclaimer does not apply to major donor committees, because they are general prrpor. committees and they do not
receive contributions fiom others.


File No. l-ll-242
smaller than 10 inches in diameter, a bumper sticker smaller than 60 square
inches, or other advertisement as determined by regulations of the
Commission." (Emphasis added.)
In addition, Regulation 18450.1 provides in part:
"(b) In addition to the exempted communications in subdivision (b) of
Section 84501, none.of the following are an .advertisement':
(2) A communication from an organization to its members, other than a
communication from a political party to its members. . . .',
This provision applies to communications where the corporation's employees,
shareholders, or their families will know that the corporation is communicating with them via the
employee newsletter, or a letter to shareholders, so an additional disclaimer sta-ting that the
communication is from the corporation would be superfluous and is not required.
7. Since the definition of adverttsement in Regulation 18450.1(a)( j) includes an
electronic message delivered to more than 200 recipients, is it correct that an electronic media
advertisement that is forwarded via an electronic message (e-mail) is not required to inclade any
disclaimers unless the electronic message is dtstributed to more than 200 reiipienx?
No, the electronic media advertisement is required to have the disclaimer specified by
Section 84506, requiring the committee name.
Section 18450.1 defines an "advertisement" in part as follows:
"(a) An advertisement as defined in section 94501 includes but is not
limited to the following:
(1) Programming received by a television or radio;
(2) A communication as described in subdivision (a) of Section 84501 that
is placed in broadcast, print or electronic media;
(A) An electronic media advertisement means an advertisement, logo, icon,
writing, image, recording, or other data transmitted, distributed, port"i,
broadcast, or displayed electronically. This includes, but is not limited to
advertisements in electronic messages, electronic message attachments, text
messages, or advertisements that appear on Intemet web pages, blogs, mobile
devices, or other electronic communication systems.
t!.). n telephone, facsimile, or electronic message that is hot solicited by the
recipient and is intended for delivery in substantially similar form to more
than200 recipients. For puqposes of this paragraph, when a committee sends
a message to 200 or fewer recipients and the message is ,.forwarded,, to other
persons by a recipient, the message sent by the committee is not an
advertisement by that committee unless the recipient forwarded the message at


File No. l-ll-242
the behest of the committee and more than 200 total recipients received the
message."
Paragraph (aX3) applies to an email communication, such as a message from a committee
that asks recipients to "vote for Measure X." such an electronic mail message, that is not
solicited by the recipient and is intended for delivery in .i urt*tially similar form to more than
200 recipients, must have a disclaimer stating the committee name.
Paragraph (a)(2)applies to an electronic media advertisement. If a committee places a
video advertisement on a website against a measure, the advertisement ir ,"q*ii.o to have a

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 13, 2012 Ms. Anita D. Stearns Mayo Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, LLP P.O. Box 7880 San Francisco, CA 94120-7880 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our file no. I-11-242 Dear Ms. Stearns Mayo: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry asks general questions of law and does not present specific facts concerning a named corporation and its expenditures on a particular ballot measure, we are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.² FACTS Pillsbury Winthrop represents numerous clients who, from time to time, make independent expenditures to support or oppose state or local ballot measures. Many of these clients are corporations which qualify as state major donor committees. When a corporation which qualifies as a state major donor committee makes independent expenditures to support or oppose a local ballot measure, the corporation becomes subject to additional disclosure and advertising disclaimer requirements. You have the following general questions concerning corporate political activity under the Act. 1. When a corporation's internal employees spend more than ten percent of their compensated time in one month engaged in political activities expressly advocating the support or opposition of a ballot measure, the corporation must report the portion of the employees' salaries spent on such activities as independent expenditures. However, the ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-11-242 Page No. 2 corporation is not required to disclose the names, addresses and the specific salaries of those employees. Although a 1979 opinion (In re Nielsen,5 FPPC Ops 18) stated that such disclosure is required, the current FPPC Campaign Disclosure Manual 6, page 3-7, provides that such itemized disclosure is not required. Regulation 18423 addresses when an employer's payments for an employee's personal services count as contributions and expenditures under the Act. Regulation 18423 provides: "(a) The payment of salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, or other compensation by an employer to an employee who spends more than 10% of his compensated time in any one month rendering services for political purposes is a contribution, as defined in Section 82015 and Regulation 18215, or an expenditure, as defined in Section 82025 and Regulation 18225, by the employer if: (1) The employee renders services at the request or direction of the employer; (2) The employee, with consent of the employer, is relieved of any normal working
